macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Four Wives and Sequential Collection with Oaths

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: Four wives married sequentially and their priority in collecting from the estate
The rule of oaths: Each wife must swear to the wives coming after her that her ketubah has not been paid
The fourth wife's exemption from oath-taking according to the standard view
Ben Nanas disagrees: even the fourth wife must take an oath due to the possibility of earlier fields being stolen
The case of documents with identical or ambiguous dates and how to determine priority

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Analysis of the Oath Debate: Tana Kama vs. Ben Nanas

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's formulation: The debate concerns whether a later wife who collects may be required to give up her field if an earlier wife's field was stolen
Tana Kama holds that if a later wife must return her field, she has no need to swear since she cannot keep it
Ben Nanas holds that the later wife may keep the field and therefore must swear to protect the interests of earlier wives
Rav Nachman reframes: Both agree the later wife must return the field if an earlier one was stolen; the debate is whether she strip-mines it
Ben Nanas: Making the oath leads the wife to believe she can keep the field, deterring her from strip-mining

macro_segments.title: The Oath and Representation: Partners in Undivided Property and Court Cases

micro_segments.title: The case of two brothers or partners where one goes to court without the other: does he implicitly represent the whole estate or only his half?
The Gemara's question: Why does wife one only have to swear to wife two, not to three and four?
Resolution: An oath made once is valid for all; one does not need to swear separately to each party
Exception: If the other partner was in town, he cannot claim non-representation; context determines implicit authority

macro_segments.title: Two Documents on the Same Day: Rav vs. Shmuel and the Underlying Principles

micro_segments.title: The case of two sale documents for the same field dated on the same day; Rav rules to divide the field
Shmuel: The judges should attempt to determine the true owner rather than compromise
Rav follows Rabbi Meir: the signatories make the document binding, so identical dates mean equal claims
Shmuel follows Rabbi Elazar: the witnesses of delivery make the document binding; one person truly owns it
Gemara resolves: Even if the true owner exists, a braita shows the position that splits the field in such cases

macro_segments.title: The Case of Rami bar Chama and Mar Ukva: Judicial Authority and Shuda De-Dayni

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama and Mar Ukva each received a document from their mother on the same day; Rav Sheshet and Rav Nachman award to different sons
Rav Nachman's position: we do not go by who received the document first, but by the date written in the document
Rav Nachman claims judicial authority (shuda de-dayni) to award it arbitrarily; Rav Sheshet challenges this on two grounds
Tosafot clarifies: Shuda de-dayni is limited to the supreme judge of the generation and only for land, not movable property
Rav Nachman's second response: You did not consciously exercise shuda de-dayni; you merely judged incorrectly that he deserved it because he received it first

macro_segments.title: The Case of Ambiguous Document Dating and Reimbursement Rights

micro_segments.title: Two documents presented to Rav Yosef: one dated the fifth of Nissan, the other simply 'Nissan' without specifying the day
Rav Yosef awards the field to the one with the specific date (fifth of Nissan)
The claimant with the ambiguous document seeks to collect reimbursement from other buyers of the seller's fields
The other buyers respond: you are the true owner, so we cannot pay you; only the awarded party can collect from us
Solution: The awarded party must grant power of attorney to the claimant so the claimant can collect reimbursement in the other's name

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Elazar on gittin and documents
Two deeds for the same property
Rami bar Chama and Mar Ukva
Written date vs. actual delivery date
